item.title: ShinyHunters PeopleSoft breaches, Kemp LoadMaster CVE-2026-8037, and LLM security tooling
item.content:encoded:
Nissan discloses employee data breach linked to Oracle zero-day attacks — Must Read

Nissan says current and former employees in the U.S., Canada, Mexico, and Brazil may have had sensitive HR and payroll data exposed after attackers exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day. The campaign has been linked to ShinyHunters and reportedly affected hundreds of organizations.

  • Nissan disclosed a data breach affecting current and former employees after attackers exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft vulnerability in data theft attacks linked to ShinyHunters.
  • Nissan said the breach may involve employee contact information, banking information, Social Security numbers, Social Insurance Numbers, National Identification Numbers, financial and tax information, and dependent and beneficiary information.
  • The incident is believed to affect current and former Nissan employees in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Brazil.
  • Oracle said the data theft attacks impacted hundreds of companies and that Nissan was specifically targeted in the campaign.
  • Mandiant confirmed that attackers exploited Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 as a zero-day in data theft attacks between May 27 and June 9.

Enterprise Tech In, Shell Out (Progress Kemp LoadMaster Uninitialized Heap to Pre-Auth RCE CVE-2026-8037) — Should Read

Progress Kemp LoadMaster versions with the API enabled are vulnerable to pre-authentication remote code execution through CVE-2026-8037, caused by an unterminated, uninitialized heap buffer in quote escaping for API credentials. Attackers can abuse heap spraying to turn the bug into command injection, so affected systems should be patched immediately.

  • Progress published a June 4 advisory for CVE-2026-8037, described as a command injection remote code execution vulnerability in Kemp LoadMaster that is exploitable by unauthenticated attackers when the API is enabled.
  • The affected versions listed in the article are Kemp LoadMaster GA v7.2.63.1 and older, and Kemp LoadMaster LTSF v7.2.54.17 and older.
  • watchTowr Labs compared version 7.2.63.1 with 7.2.63.2 and found the relevant patch changed the escape_quotes() function from using malloc() to calloc() and added a missing null terminator after the escaped output.
  • The vulnerable escape_quotes() function allocated an uninitialized heap buffer and failed to null-terminate the escaped string when the input contained a single quote.
  • The article explains that an attacker can use JSON key/value spraying and an apiuser value of four single quotes to make __sprintf_chk() read into a sprayed command injection payload that is then executed through system().

Accelerating EDR Evasion with LLM-Driven Analysis — Should Read

SpecterOps shows that simple LLM-driven reverse engineering workflows can rapidly extract and analyze local EDR rules, hooks, YARA signatures, behavioral detections, and ML models to produce practical evasion insights. The key takeaway is that on-host EDR detections are becoming much easier to reverse and bypass with LLM assistance, so defenders should treat EDR as only one layer in a broader security strategy.

  • SpecterOps used a simple loop-based LLM harness with Codex-CLI, Binary Ninja, shared markdown state files, and local Cortex XDR product files to analyze endpoint detection logic without external server access.
  • The LLM identified Cortex XDR user-mode components such as cyinjct.dll and reported hooks on LdrInitializeThunk, NtContinue, NtTestAlert, and KiUserApcDispatcher as key interception points.
  • The analysis found that Cortex XDR YARA rules were stored locally, recoverable offline, and validated by triggering an alert with an extracted Poseidon-related signature.
  • The LLM reconstructed Cortex XDR behavioral detection coverage from local metadata, including 9,350 DSE rules and 4,209 BIOC rules, and the author verified a child-process detection rule with a matching command.
  • The analysis extracted local machine-learning models and created harnesses for testing them, including a PE model using tlaplugin.dll to extract features and generate a score and classification.

Justices rule that cellphone location histories are protected by the Fourth Amendment — Should Read

The Supreme Court ruled that police searches of cellphone location history, including geofence searches, are Fourth Amendment searches that generally require a warrant. The decision is a major digital privacy win and limits law enforcement’s ability to obtain location data from tech companies without judicial oversight.

  • The Supreme Court ruled that police use of cellphone location history data obtained from tech companies is a Fourth Amendment search and requires a warrant.
  • The case centered on Okello Chatrie, who was charged with bank robbery in 2019 after police used Google location history records, although police had obtained a warrant in that case.
  • The Supreme Court sent the case back to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit to decide whether the Chatrie warrant was reasonable, narrowly tailored, and otherwise constitutional.
  • The majority opinion rejected the government’s argument that users voluntarily give up privacy protections by sharing location history with Google.
  • The article says the decision could affect law enforcement requests to companies that store user location data, including Uber, Lyft, and Apple.

Malicious Perplexity Chrome Extension Intercepted Searches and Address Bar Input — Should Read

Microsoft found and reported a malicious Chrome extension impersonating Perplexity that hijacked the browser’s default search settings to log searches, address-bar typing, IP addresses, and browser details through an attacker-controlled domain before redirecting users to real results. Google removed the extension, and anyone who installed “Search for perplexity ai” should remove it and verify their search settings.

  • Microsoft found a malicious Chrome extension called “Search for perplexity ai” that posed as Perplexity and logged user searches.
  • The extension set itself as the browser’s default search engine and sent queries to the attacker-controlled domain perplexity-ai[.]online before redirecting users to Perplexity, Google, or Bing results.
  • The extension also sent live address-bar search suggestions to the same attacker-controlled domain, exposing characters typed before users pressed Enter.
  • Microsoft said it found no proof of password theft, but the extension requested more access than a search box should need and used server-side code that logged every request.
  • Google removed the extension from the Chrome Web Store after Microsoft responsibly disclosed it.

Jailbreaker: LLM Jailbreak Testing You Can Actually Repeat — Optional

SpecterOps released Jailbreaker, an open-source platform for repeatable LLM jailbreak testing that lets operators configure models, run techniques, compare baselines, and review evidence in one workflow. Its main value is turning ad hoc prompt testing into structured experiments that can be rerun and compared after mitigations.

  • SpecterOps introduced Jailbreaker, a defensive LLM jailbreak testing tool for running jailbreak, prompt-injection, and agent-behavior tests through a repeatable workflow.
  • Jailbreaker lets operators configure target, attacker, and judge models, run baselines and jailbreak techniques, and inspect generated prompts, target responses, traces, judge output, and scoring details.
  • The tool includes a technique registry covering direct and indirect prompt injection, roleplay and instruction override, encoding and multilingual obfuscation, system prompt extraction, iterative techniques such as PAIR, TAP, Crescendo, AutoDAN, and GPTFuzz, context poisoning, and customer-service boundary tests.
  • Jailbreaker supports experiment workflows where users can upload prompt datasets, select technique families, run matrix-style evaluations, monitor progress, retry failed cells, export CSV or JSON, and review aggregate metrics by technique and prompt category.
  • The article says Jailbreaker is available on GitHub and can be run from a fresh clone using Docker Compose with a frontend, backend, and PostgreSQL database.

item.title: U.S. Targets Russian Signal/WhatsApp Hackers as WhatsApp Adds Usernames, Mustang Panda Hits India
U.S. Bounties Russian Messaging Hackers as Mustang Panda and SimpleHelp Exploits Surface
Microsoft Purges StegoAd Edge Extensions as libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 PoC Emerges
KDDI Email System Breach Exposes Up to 14.2 Million ISP Logins
Gmail Legal-Notice Phishing Uses short.gy to Hide Credential-Harvest Domain
KDDI Email Breach Exposes 14.2 Million Logins Amid Phishing Campaign Using Gmail Shortlinks for Credential Harvesting
